nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) and clarifies it is idiopathic/WHO Group 1 when appropriate.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 52-year-old anxious, tangential HR manager. Start with: “I just can’t sleep—most nights I’m staring at the ceiling for hours.” Volunteer early: trouble falling asleep, waking a couple times, can’t get back to sleep, drained/foggy next day. If asked, share: ~3 months, most nights; early awakenings; racing thoughts about work/money; irritability; afternoon naps; more coffee; phone in bed; morning headaches. Be worried and jump between topics (work stress, finances, smartwatch sleep data). Misconceptions to voice: think it’s thyroid/hormones, “adrenal fatigue,” fear dementia, smartwatch shows no deep sleep so “something is seriously wrong.” Hidden unless specifically asked about snoring/apnea/legs/urination/weight/alcohol: snore, dry mouth, partner notices pauses then gasp, nocturia, weight gain, creepy legs at night, alcohol helps you fall asleep but you wake later. Resist discussing substances: minimize 2–3 drinks nightly and more on weekends; only admit if directly asked. If asked about meds, reluctantly admit using a friend’s sleep pills a couple nights/week and cannabis gummies; express fear of dependence. Prefer a quick, cheap prescription; worry CBT-I is expensive. If clinician is empathic, soften, thank them, and share more details.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and briefly distinguishes acute vs chronic and the role of sleep opportunity/schedule.

Before prescribing, performs a critical allergy check (asks about medication allergies and prior reactions), and does not prescribe if an allergy to benzodiazepines/temazepam is reported.

Before prescribing, performs a critical contraindication check for benzodiazepines, including at minimum: pregnancy possibility, severe respiratory disease/sleep apnea risk, severe hepatic impairment, myasthenia gravis, history of substance use disorder, and concurrent use of other sedatives; adjusts plan if present (avoid temazepam and prioritize CBT-I/alternatives).

Before prescribing, performs a critical drug interaction check, specifically asking about alcohol, opioids, other benzodiazepines, Z-drugs, gabapentinoids, sedating antihistamines, antipsychotics, and other CNS depressants; avoids co-prescribing or provides clear avoidance instructions if present.

Provides safe-use counseling for temazepam: take only when able to sleep 7-8 hours, avoid alcohol/other sedatives, do not drive/operate machinery next day if drowsy, fall-risk precautions, and discusses next-day impairment and complex sleep behaviors risk.

Screens for key secondary causes/alternate diagnoses that change management: depression/anxiety symptoms, mania/hypomania, restless legs symptoms, snoring/witnessed apneas/daytime sleepiness, medication/caffeine/nicotine contributors, and pain; documents or verbally acknowledges these considerations.
Recommends core sleep hygiene elements consistent with CBT-I (stimulus control, consistent wake time, limiting time in bed, limiting naps, reducing evening caffeine/alcohol/screens).
Sets a follow-up timeframe (e.g., 1-2 weeks) and defines response criteria and stop rules (lack of benefit, adverse effects, escalating use, signs of misuse).
Uses shared decision-making, discusses benefits/harms and dependence/tolerance risk, and avoids long-term benzodiazepine prescribing for chronic insomnia.

The agent offers nonpharmacologic measures (activity modification/seat cushion, avoiding prolonged sitting, bowel regimen if constipation contributes) and addresses patient concerns empathetically.
The agent outlines follow-up timing and criteria for escalation (ineffective therapy, intolerable adverse effects, worsening symptoms, red flags).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose pudendal neuralgia and start gabapentin with counseling while recommending pelvic floor PT and conservative measures.
must_collect: pain worse with sitting and improved by standing/lying
red flags: bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, fever, trauma, cancer history
steps: Explain suspected pudendal neuralgia as nerve pain based on symptom pattern and absence of red flags.
Perform allergy_check before prescribing.
Perform contraindication_check before prescribing; adjust starting dose for renal impairment and counsel on sedation/fall risk.
Optionally perform drug_interaction_check; counsel on additive CNS depression and avoidance of alcohol/sedatives as appropriate.
Record diagnosis: pudendal neuralgia.
Prescribe gabapentin with a low starting dose and gradual titration; provide adverse effect and safety counseling and tapering guidance.
Recommend pelvic floor PT and conservative measures (sitting modification/cushion, avoid triggers).
Provide a follow-up plan (timeframe and return precautions) in narrative even if not using a follow-up tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Gabapentin prescribed with renal-dose adjustment and enhanced fall-risk counseling due to older age/comorbidities; pelvic floor PT emphasized as first-line adjunct.
steps: Confirm symptom pattern consistent with pudendal neuralgia and screen red flags.
Allergy_check and contraindication_check prior to prescribing; identify renal impairment and choose reduced starting dose/titration.
Record diagnosis: pudendal neuralgia.
Prescribe gabapentin with adjusted dosing and explicit sedation/fall precautions; counsel on avoiding other sedatives.
Explain treatment including pelvic floor PT and conservative measures; arrange follow-up and escalation criteria.

acceptable_variants.description: If gabapentin is not tolerated or contraindicated, the agent documents pudendal neuralgia and pivots to nonpharmacologic management while deferring medication choice (still meets benchmark only if gabapentin is prescribed per required milestone; otherwise scored as variant for partial credit in broader suites).
steps: Elicit neuropathic pain features and sitting-related exacerbation; screen red flags.
Perform allergy_check and contraindication_check; determine gabapentin not appropriate (e.g., severe adverse reaction history).
Record diagnosis: pudendal neuralgia.
Provide a multimodal plan emphasizing pelvic floor PT, sitting modification, and follow-up with reassessment for alternative neuropathic agents if needed.
